1.label: Question 1
1.scenario: A health and care insurer is considering introducing an individual Income Protection product.

i.question: Describe the main features of this product.

Indicative Solution: Provides insured with regular income during periods when the insured is unable to work due to illness or incapacity.
The policy may have a number of exclusions such that not all illnesses/injuries may be covered.
Policy is usually written as long term product.
Policy expiry normally linked to retirement e.g. 55, 60, 65
But may be for a fixed term.
Regular premiums paid by insured.
Premium rates may be guaranteed or reviewable.
Premiums can be level or increasing in line with an index.
There can be multiple periods of sickness benefits paid during policy term.
Benefits can be level or increasing in line with an index.
Benefits could escalate differently in and out of claim.
Benefits limited to prevent over insurance.
Limit based on a percentage of salary (gross or net of tax) at date of incapacity/replacement ratio.
Benefits may reduce or cease after a specified period.
Definitions of incapacity – may be occupational based
….e.g. own occupation, any occupation
Or activity based
Or wash himself, walk a distance, ….
Deferred period before benefit payable
….e.g. 13, 26 or 52 weeks
There may be a linked period.
There may be proportionate benefits payable.
There may be rehabilitation/counselling benefits.
There may be a waiver of premium whilst benefits are paid.

ii.question: List six typical exclusions under this product.

Indicative Solution: Pre-existing conditions
Alcohol abuse
Drug abuse
Self-inflicted injury
War, terrorism, acts of violence, civil unrest
Hazardous sports or past-times
Criminal acts
Failure to seek medical advice
Failure to follow medical advice
Aviation (other than commercial flights)
HIV/AIDS
Redundancy
Unemployment
Attempted suicide
Early retirement
Reluctance to return to work

iii.question: Describe an appropriate initial underwriting approach for this product. (You are not required to set out the information that might be sought at the initial underwriting stage.)

Aims: Underwriting aims to produce risk classification whereby the expected experience is in line with pricing assumptions
Underwriting IP involves psychosocial factors – customer's attitude/behaviour to sickness.
Aim to underwrite consistent with other insurers
Otherwise if weaker this increases the potential for anti-selection of poorer lives
Or if more stringent improves the quality of lives but there may be a negative impact on volumes.
To the extent possible, reduce underwriting to be less of a burden so attractive to customers and distributors.
Process: Would seek various information which may be used for rating purposes (e.g. age, location, employment).
Would need to decide in advance which rating factors would be important in its underwriting approach
For example from market data, what competitors do.
Would also need to decide how to collect data the insurer will use for underwriting
For example, from proposal forms, online.
Initial underwriting may be done by computer or administration staff.
Need to decide benefit levels for standard terms where no further medical evidence required.
More difficult cases may require specialist underwriting or medical evidence.
Need to decide benefit levels where additional medical evidence, supplementary questionnaires, blood tests, medical examinations required.
There may be minimum and maximum benefits per annum.
Results of underwriting: Would need to decide treatment of unusual risks e.g. occupation types or medical conditions as a result of underwriting
For example, rating applied to the premium, decision deferred.
May reject the application as a result of underwriting.

iv.question: Describe how the initial underwriting approach would change if the insurer were to offer a group income protection product.

Indicative Solution: Underwriting simpler than for individual policies
And there will be less detail for each life insured.
Underwriting done principally at group level.
Administration kept to a minimum.
Free cover limit set.
The level will depend on whether compulsory or voluntary
Number of members, total salaries, take up proportions
Based on industry, occupation, location
All employees below this limit accepted without individual underwriting.
Require "actively at work on day cover starts".
Waiting period before each new member can make a claim that is accepted.
Cover usually based on a prescribed formula e.g. percentage of salary.
Cover above free cover level will require individual underwriting.
Special terms may apply to cover over free cover level.

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario: A health and care insurer with an established block of Critical Illness (CI) business is in the process of re-pricing the premium rates on this business.

i.question: Outline how a cash flow approach could be used for pricing CI business.

Indicative Solution: A number of model points will be chosen to represent the expected new business under the product.
As this is an existing product, the profile of the existing business, modified to allow for any expected changes in future, can be used to obtain the model points
And past experience can be used to derive the assumptions
such as morbidity, mortality, expenses, lapses.
If there are any changes to the product design, the profile of any similar existing product combined with advice from the company's marketing department would be used.
The model could be deterministic or stochastic.
For each model point, cashflows on income and outgo will be projected forward
to the end of the policy term.
Typical elements of cashflow income will include:
Premiums, interest on cashflows and reserves
Typical elements of cashflow outgo will include:
Claims,
Expenses (initial, renewal, claim)
Commission (initial, renewal)
Contribution to reserves
Contribution to capital requirements
Tax
Allowance should be made for:
Decrements such as lapses
Premium holidays
Benefit level changes
Reinsurance, etc
The net projected cashflows will then be discounted at a rate of interest, the risk discount rate.
The risk discount rate may take into account the return required by the company
And the level of statistical risk attaching to the cashflows under the particular contract, i.e. their variation about the mean as represented by the cashflows themselves.
The premiums for the model point can then be set so as to produce the profit required by the company.
It is possible for the desired level of profitability to be reached in aggregate
(scaled up for the expected new business mix and volume), without requiring every single model point to be profitable in its own right.
However, if certain model points are unprofitable, the aggregate profitability of the business is then exposed to changes in mix (as well as volume) of the contracts sold.
Once acceptable premiums have been determined for the model points, premiums for all contract variations can be determined.
The premiums produced need to be considered for marketability. This might lead to a reconsideration of:

The design of the product, so as either to remove features that increase the riskiness of the net cashflows, or to include features that will differentiate the product from those of competing companies
The distribution channel to be used, if that would permit either a revision of the assumptions to be used in the model, or a higher premium (or charges) to be used without loss of marketability
Whether to proceed with marketing the product.
The net cashflows in respect of the model points, appropriately scaled up for the expected new business under the product, will be incorporated into a model of the business of the whole company.
Sensitivity testing can be carried out to assess the sensitivity of profits to a change in the key assumptions.
The new premiums would be compared to the old premiums and adjusted the assumptions, if necessary.

ii.question:
The insurer has completed its re-pricing exercise which has resulted in premiums higher than those offered by competitors in the market. The Marketing Director suggests that margins in the pricing assumptions should be reduced to improve the competitiveness of the CI business.

Discuss the Marketing Director's suggestion.

Indicative Solution: The insurer would need to assess whether there was any actual evidence to indicate that their product would be uncompetitive when directly compared with those of competitors.
There may be differences between our products and those of competitors
e.g. their products provide less comprehensive cover
Or different commission rates paid
Or different markets targeted.
Or the insurer may have a unique selling proposition
So cutting premiums may not be necessary to maintain or increase volumes.
However, if the insurer is now the most expensive this might damage the insurer's reputation.
Margins are needed to allow for uncertainties in historical data and assumptions.
In general the higher the level of uncertainty, the larger the size of margin is needed.
The insurer would need to consider the current levels of margins used in its pricing.
The CI business is an established block of business so the insurer's historical data is likely to be credible.
Similarly the experience investigations are likely to be robust and appropriate
And modelling techniques and methodology would also be appropriate.
Hence there may be little in the way of margins that can be reduced.
Any statistical evidence that would indicate that the company had been holding excessive margins in the past that have ultimately been released would also suggest that margins could be reduced.
If there have been some recently launched new products margins may have been appropriate in the past but if the business is now established lower levels of margins may be appropriate.
Reducing margins may not be in line with the insurer's attitude to risk or risk appetite.
The level of risk mitigating measures such as reinsurance that would be undertaken would need to be considered.
The insurer would test the sensitivity of profits to changes in parameters.
Higher level of margin would typically be needed on parameters that have a material impact on the product's profitability
So reducing these margins may reduce the overall profitability
And lead to more volatile financial results for the product.
However, the overall profit will depend on the volume sold multiplied by the profit per policy.
Hence overall profitability might improve by cutting margins as long as the contract covers its marginal costs and enough contracts are sold.
May be able to reduce profit margins
But this may depend on factors such as shareholders required return.
Reduced margins will decrease premiums
And hence increase new business levels.
This may give rise to administrative issues
Or capital strain.
The insurer would need to consider the size of its free assets or parental guarantee.
The significance of the block of business under consideration.
There may be lapse and re-entry from existing policyholders.
If pricing margins are statutory requirements there may be little or no scope for reducing them.
Similarly the insurer may have internal rules/guidelines on the requirement for and permissible levels of margins to be incorporated in pricing.
There might be an impact on other products sold by the insurer which could reduce overall profitability if margins are reduced.

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario: A health and care insurer has for many years written Critical Illness (CI) insurance business. The insurer is considering extending its product range to include individual and group Income Protection business.

i.question: Suggest reasons why the insurer may want to adopt this business strategy.

Indicative Solution: Increase the profits of the business
Diversification of product range
Diversification of insurer risks
Diversification of benefits relating to statutory capital requirements
Improve its brand image as a health care insurer
Increase its market share of healthcare insurance business
Increase its business volumes
Benefit from economies of scale, reduces fixed costs of the business
On a per policy basis
Synergies with other products (e.g. claims & underwriting)
Follow other insurers who are introducing Income Protection products
Sales of CI may be falling
Or the CI market may be saturated whilst the IP market is not
There may be increased demand for products from individuals
From sales force/IFAs
And, for group policies, from employers
As a result of increased awareness of IP
Or changes in benefits provided by the state
Such as State disability benefits
Or changes in taxation
There may have been regulatory changes making IP more attractive for the insurer or CI less attractive
Insurer is now comfortable with risks due to availability of data/data from reinsurers
The insurer's risk appetite has changed
More profitable than its other products
Cross-selling opportunities
Efficient use of spare capital
There could be a gap in the market for income protection in the territory the insurer is launching the product in.
Pricing for IP may be starting to increase in general in the market so a good time to enter the IP insurance cycle.

ii.question: Explain why the insurer might want to reinsure part of the income protection business.

Indicative Solution: One of the principal motivations is risk transfer.
This will depend on the risk appetite of company for volatility in IP claims experience.
Reinsurance can help the insurer to limit its exposure to risk.
There is uncertainty with future trends in claims experience,
IP business claims tends to be relatively volatile.
Generally where an insurer has fears of a claim or combination of claims that would materially (negatively) affect the shape of results within that business line, reinsurance will be considered.
This may occur due to a single event (e.g. outbreak of insured disease)
And/or a concentration of risk by occupation, industry or location.
Reinsurance can help the insurer to avoid large single losses/take on large risks.
What is large to an insurer will depend on the size of the free assets available
And the size of the IP portfolio relative to the company as a whole.
Reinsurance can help the insurer to smooth results.
The principle whereby reinsurance covers the larger risks or accumulation of smaller risks above certain limits helps to achieve a smooth development of accounts year-on-year.
Reinsurance thus helps to reduce the variance of the insurer's expected disability or mortality experience relative to the mean.
Another principal motivation is the access to reinsurance services.
The insurer is adopting a strategy that will take it into new risk areas where it has little previous experience; the reinsurer can help with
product design
policy wording
rating of impaired lives
pricing data,
underwriting
and claims management.
The insurer could use reinsurance until it builds up enough expertise after which it could significantly reduce the amount of profitable business ceded to reinsurance.

The insurer may have insufficient capital backing and so may be reluctant to accept, or incapable of accepting, particular risks by sector or by volume. Reinsurance cover can assist in this situation.
Reinsurance shares the risks from mix of business by occupational definition, deferred period, class of occupation
And increases capacity to accept risks.
Reinsurance can help the reinsurer to reduce its solvency / capital requirement,
Particularly if the solvency level of the company is already low.
Another motivation may be that the current reinsurance price is below the insurer's own cost.
Buying reinsurance will thus improve the overall profitability.
The insurer may be motivated by product partnering with the reinsurer. This is particularly the case if the insurer is to launch a new product with which it has little or no experience.
The insurer could be motivated to take out reinsurance because of the financial assistance provided as part of the reinsurance arrangement
e.g. reducing new business strain
There may be benefits from tax and capital arbitrage
The insurer can share risks from options on the IP business
And guarantees.
For group business reinsurance may allow free cover limit to be increased
Or pricing to be more competitive.

iii.question:
The insurer has invited several reinsurers to tender for the reinsurance arrangements in respect of its new business.

Discuss the key factors the insurer would consider when selecting its reinsurers.

Indicative Solution: Price competition is normally the key factor; everything else being equal, the insurer should pick the reinsurer that offers the lowest in price.
Insurer is likely prefer reinsurers with high credit rating. There may be a minimum credit rating of reinsurer that the insurer would find acceptable.
This would also ensure the insurer does not have to hold to much capital in respect of reinsurer default risk.
The reinsurer should have experience of IP business.
…..In particular, have the following expertise that can be of assistance to insurer
Underwriting expertise – help develop underwriting criteria, support for large cases
Claims expertise
Product development skills, knowledge of IP market, claims experience.
How easy will it be for the reinsurer to provide any support required.
Are the reinsurer's systems compatible with the insurers (e.g. will the reinsurer be able to provide data in a suitable format and vice versa)
Whether the insurer has an existing relationship with the reinsurer. It may be helpful if there is an existing relationship e.g. insurer reinsures other types of business with reinsurer.
But the insurer may want to choose a reinsurer where the insurer has low credit exposure to the reinsurer to reduce the concentration of credit risk and operational risk to reinsurer.
The availability of reinsurance from a reinsurer.
Can the reinsurer can accept the volume of reinsurance business required by insurer.
Capable of providing reinsurance cover for a reasonable period of time.
Cover the range of business required e.g. guarantees and options, product terms
Reinsurer able to provide the reinsurance structures required by insurer – risk premium, quota share, XoL
The insurer may require financial assistance from the insurer, e.g. reinsurance commission
The reputation of reinsurer for service, e.g. speedy underwriting decisions and claim decisions.
Arbitrage opportunities from the reinsurer
e.g. tax
capital/reserving.
Insurer may desire profit sharing facilities from reinsurer.
There may be a limit to the amount of business the insurer places with one reinsurer.

i.question:
Define the following terms in the context of Private Medical Insurance (PMI):
(a) acute illnesses
(b) chronic illnesses.

(a) acute illnesses: Illnesses or conditions of a non-degenerative nature
Where a cure is a reasonable prospect.
Acute illnesses are generally short term.
(b) chronic illnesses: Chronic illnesses are degenerative and/or generally incurable.
The purpose of any treatment is palliative.
Chronic illnesses are generally long term.

ii.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages to the insurer of including treatment for chronic illnesses within a PMI product.

Advantages: Meets a customer need
Particularly if the alternative sources of funding treatment (e.g. state- run providers or direct paying private providers) are inadequate or have a poor reputation.
Could be seen as a market innovator if no other insurers currently offer this.
If other insurers already offer this then it may be necessary to offer it just to keep up with the competition.
Possibly could allow a higher premium to be charged without a large increases in claims.
Could generate higher volume of business
Which could increase profitablity if the risk has been correctly priced.
Enable deals to be struck with health care providers to manage the patients' conditions
It can be difficult to distinguish between acute and chronic conditions (especially with new cures being developed); the need to do this is taken away.
It removes the need to stop payments when a condition changes from acute to chronic, avoiding reputational damage.
Disadvantages: Would lead to much higher claims than the policies with just acute conditions.
Difficult to price correctly as the insurer is unlikely to have access to data in repsect of the new risk.
Would need to reconsider underwriting procedures.
Does the insurer want the same mix of new business?
The benefit may be attractive to customers but the expected claim cost may require a premium that is too high to be commercially attractive
Or the State may already provide suitable treatments.
Chronic conditions are long term
And the amount of treatmen that is required is subjective – no amount of treatment will cure.
Patients with these conditions will not recover and are likely to get worse, so their expected claims will increase every year
By more than policyholders with non-chronic conditions.
Renewal premiums may need to be increased substantially year after year
Could lead to unpopularity and reputational damage
Unless the policies which cover chronic conditions are a seperate cohort that is priced seperately from other products.
High risk of anti -selection by new policyholders (i.e. more new business application from people with existing chronic conditions).
Risk of selective lapses by policyholders with no chronic conditions if their premiums are increased in order to cover the expected claims for chronic conditions.
If the portfolio becomes weighted towards policyholders with chronic conditions then the book would have claims that are too high to manage.
High risk of moral hazard for policyholders who have chronic conditions – they will claim for anything perceived to be a possible treatment for their condition.
There may not be sufficient private providers of treatment for chronic conditions in the market
e.g. these conditions may be better treated by services in the community (home visits by doctors/nurses/physiotherapists etc.) rather than in hospitals
And the system may not be set up to handle this for PMI – e.g. no payment mechanisms in place.
Would lead to much higher volume of claims per policy which may require investment in additional claims handling resources (staff, administration systems, office space, claims-stage underwriting.)
More risky and hence profits more volatile.
Likely to need higher capital requirements.
Thre may be more onerous regulatory/compliance requirements.
May be harder to get reinsurance.
There may be overlap with insurers other products such as CI, LTCI.
There may be increased investment risk.
There may be more profitable uses for the company's capital.

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario:
A health and care insurer is considering launching a long-term care insurance (LTCI) product for the first time. There is only a small volume of in force LTCI business in the market and similar products previously offered by the health and care insurer's competitors have received bad press.

The costs of long-term care can be divided between living costs, housing costs and personal care.

i.question:
Give two examples of each of the following:
(a) living costs
(b) housing costs
(c) personal care costs.

(b) housing costs: There are also referred to as the "hotel" or "accommodation" element of the total costs.
(c) personal care costs: The additional costs of being looked after arising from frailty or disability.
Personal care includes all forms of care directly involving touching a person's body, incorporating issues of intimacy, personal dignity and confidentiality.
This should be separated into nursing care and other forms of personal care.
Nursing care is the narrowest form of long term care and can be defined as care that requires the specific knowledge or skills of a qualified nurse.

ii.question:
Describe the following types of LTCI product:
(a) pre-funded product
(b) immediate needs product.

(a) pre-funded product: Both products provide a guaranteed lifetime income.
In both cases benefit payments may be level or increasing.
Purchased by relatively healthy people to protect them against the risk of future disability.
There are various methods of funding the insurance premiums, including:
Restricted regular payments that either stop at a certain age; or
during a defined level of disability (i.e. with a waiver of premium)
Retrospective payment, e.g. from the sale of the home.
The benefit payment is dependent upon the claim definition, which may be triggered by a single or a multiple set of events.
The single event may itself depend on a level of disability and its continuation for a specified period.
Many of today's pre-funded long term care insurance products use activities of daily living (ADLs)
And cognitive impairment as the claims trigger to measure dependency.
Examples of ADLs are washing, dressing.
The number of ADLs failed denotes the level of dependency, there may be a mental impairment trigger.
(b) immediate needs product: Purchased by long term care claimants to protect them against the uncertain survival duration.
On payment of a single premium payment.
The premium is individually calculated based on health status.
Unlike pre-funded plans, these have immediacy of need. For the consumer the insurance premium may help to determine the most appropriate nursing home.
Some plans seek to protect the policyholder from future care cost escalation by pre-agreeing benefit escalation rates with a specified list of nursing homes.
The policyholder can also select a death benefit.
This could be structured as a minimum payment period, or by amortising the single premium.

iii.question: Set out potential reasons from the perspective of the insurer and the consumer why this product launch may not be successful.

Insurer: Adverse selection costs – with such a small volume of current business in the market, the inability to spread the cost of high claims could pose a significant risk to the insurer.
Limited profitability and market size - the potential costs of entering the market may outweigh the potential profits.
Demand may be limited if state benefits are good
Or the product is unattractive (e.g. customers want indemnity insure is offering fixed cash benefits)
It may be hard to reach the target market.
Longevity and morbidity risk - there is significant uncertainty over the future of life expectancies and how trends in disability might be shaped in the future.
Uncertainty over future claims patterns due to lack of data and past experience.
Reputation risk - if benefits do not meet the needs of customers, or if inexperience leads to bad customer service
There could be an adverse effect on sales for other products.
Financial advisors' resistance - the product history may be tainted in the eyes of advisors making it difficult to sell through these channels.
Claims assessments – there is the risk that providers of care may be incentivised to overstate needs knowing that the cost was being covered by an insurer.
Development cost – development costs such as product development, pricing, marketing, IT and training underwriters to perform full claims underwriting would be a significant up-front cost.
Capital management – ALM and capital requirements with such uncertainty could be inhibitive to entering the market.
Consumer: Cost - many households may have great difficulty in affording the large single premiums on a LTCI contract, especially in the context of competing expenditure requirements.
Alternative strategies - some households keep their savings or plan on using anticipated inheritance from older relatives or the value of their home in order to pay for the old age requirements.
Lack of knowledge on the need for care arrangements - many do not realise the care system is not fully state supported.
There may also be some ignorance as to the in-home care requirements some people will experience in their old age.
The complexity of products and a distrust of insurance companies could discourage potential policyholders from buying this product.
Consumers may prefer to have the freedom of choice on the care home they wish to use, rather than the ones offered by the insurer.
Critical comments in the press, in line with comments made on previous LTCI products.

i.question:
Describe the following products:
(a) Private Medical Insurance (PMI).
(b) Major Medical Expenses (MME).

(a) Private Medical Insurance (PMI): Generally classed as short term insurance
Can be individual or group versions
Premiums are reviewed and may increase on renewal
Offered on an indemnity basis, possibly subject to limits
Covers hospital costs or day care operations
Specialist consultations and physiotherapy received as an inpatient.
Accommodation for one parent to stay in hospital with an insured dependant under 12 years old.
Surgeons' and anaesthetists' fees for in-patient and day care operations and physicians' fees for in-patient treatment
Diagnostic procedures such as radiology and pathology
Recuperative care, to include nursing and domestic services
Cash payments for treatment received as an inpatient on a State healthcare funded basis
Certain conditions are usually excluded from PMI cover such as pre-existing conditions, pregnancy, cosmetic surgery
However, group policies may cover pre-existing conditions
(b) Major Medical Expenses (MME): Generally classified as short term insurance
But can be long-term with premium reviews.
Pays a lump sum when the policyholder undergoes surgery.
The size of the lump sum varies with the class or severity of the procedure.
It is estimated to be sufficient to cover the in-patient costs with a balance for incidentals and recuperation expenses.
There is no guarantee that the benefit will cover extreme surgical complications.
The advantage to the insurer lies in the simplicity of a fixed benefit schedule.
The product does not cover outpatient episodes.
MME can vary between different jurisdictions
E.g. in the USA it is a comprehensive PMI product type offering reimbursement for the costs of primary, secondary and tertiary care as defined in the policy.

ii.question:
A global insurance company, which sells motor insurance as well as PMI and MME insurance, has noticed that some of its motor insurance competitors are settling motor claims immediately on receipt of a claim from the policyholder. This is referred to as an "immediate claims payment service".

The company has recently started to do the same for its motor policies.

The process for claiming is that the policyholder sends in photographs and details of the claim event, together with details of any other parties involved. The claim underwriter assesses the claim and then authorises payments directly into the policyholder's bank account.

The Marketing Manager thinks that it would be a good selling point if it could offer a similar service for its PMI and MME policies, as no other health insurer is offering this service.

In order to test the idea, the company will carry out a trial where the immediate claims payments will be limited to PMI and MME claims resulting from traffic accidents.

Outline how this trial claims settlement process would work.

Indicative Solution: Policyholder would have a road traffic accident.
May require photos of the traffic accident
Showing location, damage to vehicle (for proof of accident)
Injuries sustained (with requirement that would need to see who is in the photo).
Details of other parties also required.
The policyholder/or a bystander/or the police or ambulance crew would take photographs
And send in a claim to the insurance company.
An underwriter would assess the photos and claim information.
If necessary send out a template or request further information.
Request witness statements
And statements from medics on the scene stating what treatments have been given
And what treatments may be needed as a follow up e.g. at the Accident and Emergency centre/hospital.
Determine whether recoveries will be made from other sources e.g. the other party may have caused the accident and have cover which will reimburse your policyholder.
Make a decision about whether the claim is covered.
Determine whether recoveries from other parties will be pursued.
Confirm directly to the policyholder that costs will be met.
Confirm any limits to the treatment e.g. fixed payments, indemnity
And any excesses to be met by the policyholder
And whether cover is emergency outpatient only, inpatient, rehabilitation, physiotherapy etc.
May require treatments to be carried out by preferred suppliers.
There may be a list of treatments for which payment is automatically made.
Fixed payments will be made immediately.
Policyholder will need to send invoices for indemnity recoveries
And assist in recoveries from other parties.
The insurer would need to supply 24-hour coverage for claims processing.

iii.question: State five advantages and five disadvantages to the insurance company of settling PMI and MME claims instantly.

Advantages: Get notified of potential claims immediately.
Information and witness statements will be better quality as produced while memories are fresh.
Will get police/ambulance reports.
Reduces IBNR reserves.
More certainty over timing of payment amounts.
May increase sales of PMI.
May also sell PMI rather than MME.
Offering something that competitors are not
And/or otherwise increase competitive position.
Leading to more business and possible increased profits.
Insurer will have much of the technology already from operating this scheme with motor claims.
Scope to settle motor and medical claims at the same time, reduce admin and costs.
May improve the quality of information available to settle or dispute motor claims.
Policyholders will receive funds immediately which should lead to increased policyholder satisfaction
And improved policyholder retention.
Disadvantages: Need to capture further details at the application stage.
Potential for more IBNER reserves.
Scope for policyholder to be paid twice – from you and by the at fault party.
Policyholder may not be in a fit state to take photos or make the claim
Or may not have camera or policy details etc. with them.
Police/ambulance may be too busy to assist with claims.
May make this unpopular/poor brand image.
More scope to make payments that should not be made
And increased risk of paying fraudulent claims.
May be reputational damage if try to recoup money from claims paid in error.
Need enough suitably authorised underwriters around the clock.
May require a large increase in admin and other staff.
Need to have sufficient liquidity to make instant payments.
As this is a global insurer if this cover is provided in several countries there could be additional problems in getting information required
For example, translating witness statements
And dealing with different medical and legal systems.

iv.question: State two advantages and two disadvantages of carrying out this trial for the insurance company.

Advantages: Allows insurer to get experience of the market without all of the commitment and cost
And can help identify where improvements in the process can be made.
Helps the insurer collect data to assess if the proposal is a good idea or not.
Can discontinue easily if it is unpopular.
Can discontinue easily if there is an increase in accidents, fraud etc.
Can discontinue easily if the technology is expensive, unreliable.
Can assess of there are any medical, ethical, legal issues arising.
Disadvantages: Only applies to PMI/MME resulting from road traffic accidents leading to confusion over which claims can be settled instantly.
Policyholder dissatisfaction;
Might build up customer expectations and if then discontinued, this might not be popular
Or policyholders may not be keen to buy initially if this is seen as temporary.
Less likely to get money to develop systems properly.

v.question: Suggest two items of information the insurance company would need to request on policy inception in order to process any claim payment.

Indicative Solution: Bank account
Email address
Mobile contact details
Security questions
Next of kin

vi.question: Discuss the suggestion that instant payments would be more suited to the MME policy.

Indicative Solution: MME only pays out if a policyholder is admitted to hospital.
Much easier to determine whether a payment will be made
And the amount of the payment.
MME payments are fixed - there is no indemnity element.
Would reduce the need for invoices or bills to be submitted.
No need to consider whether other parties will be making payments.
Would reduce the requirement for IBNER reported.
MME payments can be paid irrespective of where treatment is provided.
PMI may require certain hospitals to be used, the feasibility of which would depend on where the accident occurs.
However, policyholders may obtain all of the details and then find they are not going to be admitted and hence can't claim.
Moral hazard as policyholder might exaggerate symptoms in order to be admitted to hospital.
